children.name: Stercorarius
children.description: Piratical seabirds that relentlessly pursue other birds until they drop or disgorge their food. The Long-tailed Skua breeds on Swedish mountain tundra; the others are passage migrants offshore. Dark and pale morphs occur in several species.

children.name: Stercorarius parasiticus
children.description: An agile piratical seabird that harries terns and small gulls until they drop their catch, occurring in both dark and pale morphs. It breeds on Swedish coastal heaths and Baltic islands.

children.name: Stercorarius pomarinus
children.description: A heavy, barrel-chested skua whose breeding success on the high-Arctic tundra rises and falls with lemming cycles. Adults sport distinctive twisted, spoon-shaped central tail feathers and pass Swedish coasts on migration.

children.name: Stercorarius longicaudus
children.description: The most graceful and tern-like of the skuas, with long streaming central tail feathers, breeding on the Swedish mountain tundra. In good lemming years it nests abundantly; in poor years many birds skip breeding entirely.

children.name: Stercorarius skua
children.description: A massive, bulky, brown skua powerful enough to attack and kill other seabirds, breeding in the North Atlantic. It passes the Swedish west coast on migration, especially in autumn gales.

children.name: Stercorarius maccormicki
children.description: One of the most southerly-breeding birds on earth, nesting on the Antarctic continent itself and recorded closer to the South Pole than any other bird. A pale-headed form of the large skua group, it is a formidable predator and kleptoparasite at penguin and petrel colonies. After breeding, South Polar Skuas undertake one of the most remarkable migrations in the bird world, crossing the equator to winter in the North Pacific and North Atlantic — some individuals have been documented travelling 14,000 km to wintering grounds in the northern oceans. Named after Robert McCormick, a British naval surgeon who sailed on Antarctic expeditions.

children.name: Stercorarius antarcticus
children.description: A heavily built skua of sub-Antarctic islands and the Antarctic Peninsula, closely related to the Great Skua but occupying the southern hemisphere. Uniformly dark brown with large white wing flashes, it is an aggressive predator at seabird colonies, taking penguin eggs, chicks, and even adult petrels. Breeds on the Falkland Islands, South Georgia, Kerguelen, and the coasts of Antarctica, often nesting near penguin rookeries to exploit them as a food source. Unlike the South Polar Skua, it rarely ventures far into the northern hemisphere during the austral winter.

children.name: Stercorarius chilensis
children.description: The only large skua endemic to South America, breeding along the coasts of Chile and Argentina south to Tierra del Fuego. Distinctive among the large skuas for its rufous-washed underparts and sides of neck, which contrast with the darker upperparts and cap. It pursues other seabirds kleptoparasitically and also scavenges and preys on small birds and rodents on shore. Unlike its Antarctic relatives it is primarily a resident of temperate and cold-temperate South American coasts, though some birds disperse northward along the Pacific coast of South America after breeding.
